string_table.string: Use the w, a, s, and d keys to move around the 360 degree image. Press the tab key to jump to interactive markers and hotspots.
To watch this video, use the preview feature in Storyline 360, upload your published output to a server, publish to Review 360, or switch to static video quality.
Streaming video is unavailable for local playback.
You must complete the question before submitting.

slidelink.displaytext: The Advantages of Building a High Functioning Team
Understanding the Basic Needs of your
Understanding the Basic Needs of your
Let's see what you have learned

search.slidetext: building high-performing teams

meet julia! join us on her journey as the new team member of a group of editors. let’s see where this course takes you hello my name is julia build trust & values define roles & responsibilities resolve conflicts manage team delegation

thought point thought point you are right if you are thinking about her ability to get everyone cooperating. but building a cohesive team is much more than that, which you’ll learn soon. we will introduce you to the foundational concepts of driving a high-functioning team throughout this course. how is julia going to contribute to building a high-performing team?

course format: areas of focus areas of focus it is important to note that building high-performing teams is a multifaceted process that requires understanding five areas of focus. team building fundamentals core requirements for success navigating conflict: stages & resolution strategies leadership empowerment strategies course assessment
what is the importance of building a high-performing team? why building a high-performing team?

the advantages of building a high functioning team high-performing teams can be defined as a group of people with specific roles and complementary skills, committed to a common purpose. team members are able to interchange their roles, have robust methods of resolving conflicts, and learn from each other. cohesive teams build strong relationships and consistently show a high level of collaboration, and innovation. they are also highly focused on their goals and achieve superior results. being part of such a team boosts job satisfaction, as everyone feels truly valued, connected, and empowered, working efficiently with minimal conflicts.

contribution everyone seeks a sense of impact the drive to reach one’s full potential (maslow’s self-actualization) we want to give beyond ourselves growth some resist growth continuous improvement of skills to push boundaries and excel love & connection the need for support by a community belonging drives performance people want to be valued by colleagues significance we seek meaning & value people want to make a difference in their career variety some uncertainty is beneficial lead projects, not tasks the need for change & challenge certainty job security consistent salary company stability comfort understanding the basic needs of your team abraham maslow’s hierarchy of needs tony robins 1 1 1 2 2 2 3 3 3 4 4 4 5 5 5 6 6 6 tony robins select each number to learn more.

contribution everyone seeks a sense of impact the drive to reach one’s full potential (maslow’s self-actualization) we want to give beyond ourselves growth some resist growth continuous development of skills to push boundaries and excel love & connection the need for support by a community belonging drives performance people want to be valued by colleagues significance members seek meaning & value they want to make a difference in their career variety the need for change & challenge some uncertainty is beneficial lead projects, not tasks certainty job security consistent salary company stability 1 1 1 2 2 2 3 3 3 4 4 4 5 5 5 6 6 6

even the most talented and well-intentioned teams can encounter challenges that hinder their performance. understanding these struggles is the first step in overcoming them. teams can struggle click the next button to learn about the five stages teams go through.
select the play icon to watch a video introducing the stages team go through. understanding team development

the final stage occurs when the team disbands after completing its objectives. members may experience feelings of accomplishment or sadness about leaving the group. this is where the team reaches peak efficiency, confidently tackling challenges, meeting goals, and delivering results. everyone knows their role, and they work seamlessly as a unit. members collaborate seamlessly, focus on achieving their objectives, and resolve conflicts quickly and effectively. trust and interdependence are high. at this stage, the group begins to develop norms, roles, and mutual respect. people recognize and appreciate each other’s strengths. members align on shared goals and processes, fostering collaboration and cohesion. this is the stage where reality sets in. challenges emerge, personalities clash, and tensions can rise. it’s a critical stage where growing pains are normal—but it’s also where the foundation for collaboration begins. team members begin to express their opinions, and differences in personalities, work styles, and priorities become apparent. this is the honeymoon phase. the team is just getting started—everyone’s polite, enthusiastic, and eager to dive into the project. this is the initial stage where team members meet, establish goals, and begin to understand their roles. conflicts happen people hold differing views about what should be done and by whom. it isn’t a matter of whether conflicts will happen, but when. conflicts aren’t always bad. they can sometimes bring innovative solutions to challenging problems. the key is managing it constructively, to everyone’s benefit.

when you combine your team’s diverse perspectives, you unlock the collective potential. by leveraging critical thinking, this approach allows members to and go beyond what any individual could achieve alone. collaborative problem solving requires an open mind, and the ability to analyze objectively. managing conflicts equally important is the ability to stay grounded in the present. by concentration on the current challenges, we open ourselves up to innovative solutions and create the foundation for a more productive workflow. managing conflicts when resolving conflicts, it is important to focus on the problem, not the people. by zeroing in on the problem rather than getting caught up in personalities, you can foster an objective dialogue that generates effective solutions. this approach ensures that the focus remains on addressing challenges rather than assigning blame. managing conflicts managing conflicts focus on the issue focus on the present and the future search for solutions together select each icon to learn more about each.

dealing with an underachieving team is your team individually capable of achieving great things but is yet to unlock their full potential as a group? managing a team that is underperforming can be both frustrating and challenging. it’s essential that you notice any underperformance and take the necessary initiatives to address it.

provide coaching through regular, constructive feedback and personalized support. coaching helps team members identify strengths, overcome challenges, and hold themselves accountable to shared goals. remember that coaching is woven into everyday interactions. whether through quick check-ins, mentoring relationships, or collaborative problem-solving, ongoing coaching turns challenges into learning opportunities. help the team develop a sense of ownership over the projects and responsibilities.to foster this mindset, involve everyone in setting common objectives and strategic decision-making, ensuring that each person’s ideas and efforts are valued. when your team truly owns their work together, they become more engaged, resilient, and innovative—driving not only better results but also a stronger, more cohesive culture. develop a social calendar to foster proactive team building. many organizations may skip this step until morale is low. by planning fun competitive activities or outing every month or two you create moments for friendly shared memories that not only strengthen team bonds but also help reduce staff turnover. do not reacting to low morale, be proactive and let regular social events become a cornerstone of your team’s success. establish a robust onboarding process to seamlessly integrate new members into your team. many organizations overlook this step, leaving newcomers struggling to connect with the existing team dynamic. when a new member joins, ensure they are promptly introduced to the entire team and clearly briefed on their responsibilities and role. start by establishing common goals and values. gather your team to collectively develop clear goals and measurable objectives that each member of your team can contribute to. you should also define a set of core values that will guide daily behavior, such as “we keep our promises”, or “we continually improve our processes.” a proactive approach with teambuilding select each icon to learn more. common goals & values common goals & values common goals & values onboarding process onboarding process onboarding process social calendar social calendar social calendar collective ownership collective ownership collective ownership coaching coaching coaching

maslow’s hierarchy maslow, a. h. (1970). motivation and personality (2nd ed.). new york: harper & row maslow’s hierarchy of human needs outlines the stages of human motivation from survival to self-actualization. survival safety belonging importance self-actualization disengaged not engaged almost engaged engaged highly engaged

engage emotionally engage emotionally analyse your emotions and take measured action analyse your emotions and take measured action build empathy. listen actively. build empathy. listen actively. build empathy. listen actively. think before you act. build trust think before you act. build trust think before you act. build trust self-regulation self-regulation self-regulation self-regulation empathy empathy empathy empathy self-awareness self-awareness self-awareness self-awareness social-awareness social-awareness social-awareness social-awareness select each area to learn more. emotional intelligence (ei) emotional intelligence (ei) refers to the ability to recognize, understand, manage, and influence emotions—both your own and those of others. emotional intelligence (ei) is necessary to: enhance leadership and team collaboration promote interpersonal understanding encourage personal growth and self-confidence emotional intelligence (ei) requires you to: select the view characteristics button to see the attributes of emotionally intelligent individuals. increase self-awareness consider the feelings of others act accordingly view characteristics view characteristics view characteristics view characteristics view characteristics view characteristics

the manager provides the context and necessary parameters, allowing the team to make decisions within those limits. this approach maximizes team autonomy and can lead to innovative solutions, as long as there is trust and accountability in place. the manager suggests a tentative decision and invites feedback to refine it further. this collaborative approach allows the team to influence the final outcome, fostering a sense of ownership while ensuring that the decision remains well-informed. the manager presents a set of options, inviting the team to discuss and weigh in while you retain the final authority. this method helps align the team’s perspectives with the leader’s vision. the manager decides and explains the reasons for the decision and the benefits of doing so. this balance can improve decision quality while still maintaining efficiency. the manager decides and tells the team what to do. the team has no active part in the decision-making process. this approach works well when quick, decisive action is needed, but it can limit creativity and team engagement. balancing team control and freedom autocratic leadership consultative leadership collaborative leadership delegative leadership fully empowered team (robert tannenbaum and warren schmidt
let’s see what you’ve learned. let’s see what you’ve learned.

incorrect that is incorrect. please try again. try again try again try again try again try again try again incorrect you did not select the correct response. continue continue continue continue continue continue correct that's right! you selected the correct response. continue continue continue continue continue continue true true true true true true false false false false false false knowledge check - 1 tom prefers not to work on the detailed tasks that are assigned, so he convinces others to do his work. everyone is fed up but the manager tells them to ignore tom. this is a good example of emotional intelligence. select true or false, and then select submit.

correct that's right! you selected the correct response. continue continue continue continue continue continue incorrect you did not select the correct response. continue continue continue continue continue continue a manager notices that employees seem anxious and unmotivated after recent company restructuring. some team members have expressed concern about job security and changes in leadership. what would be the best approach for the manager to address this issue? select the best course of action, then select submit. knowledge check - 2 tell employees to focus on their tasks and avoid discussing company changes. tell employees to focus on their tasks and avoid discussing company changes. tell employees to focus on their tasks and avoid discussing company changes. tell employees to focus on their tasks and avoid discussing company changes. tell employees to focus on their tasks and avoid discussing company changes. tell employees to focus on their tasks and avoid discussing company changes. provide regular updates on the company’s direction and job stability to reduce uncertainty. provide regular updates on the company’s direction and job stability to reduce uncertainty. provide regular updates on the company’s direction and job stability to reduce uncertainty. provide regular updates on the company’s direction and job stability to reduce uncertainty. provide regular updates on the company’s direction and job stability to reduce uncertainty. provide regular updates on the company’s direction and job stability to reduce uncertainty. increase workload to distract employees from their concerns. increase workload to distract employees from their concerns. increase workload to distract employees from their concerns. increase workload to distract employees from their concerns. increase workload to distract employees from their concerns. increase workload to distract employees from their concerns. avoid discussing the restructuring until it is finalized to prevent rumors. avoid discussing the restructuring until it is finalized to prevent rumors. avoid discussing the restructuring until it is finalized to prevent rumors. avoid discussing the restructuring until it is finalized to prevent rumors. avoid discussing the restructuring until it is finalized to prevent rumors. avoid discussing the restructuring until it is finalized to prevent rumors.

incorrect you did not select the correct response. continue continue continue continue continue continue correct that's right! you selected the correct response. continue continue continue continue continue continue knowledge check - 3 can you match each team formation stage with the appropriate management strategy? drag the correct stage label next to the management action that best fits that stage. once you have finished, select submit. promoting open communication & delegating tasks to build team capability. prioritizing individual development & addresses factors that influence team success. establishing processes & structure, smoothing conflicts & building strong relationships. allocate time to deal with unresolved team issues. clarifies the team’s purpose, sets objectives, defines external relationships. forming storming performing

incorrect that is incorrect. please try again. try again try again try again try again try again try again incorrect you did not select the correct response. continue continue continue continue continue continue correct that's right! you selected the correct response. continue continue continue continue continue continue knowledge check - 4 which of the following are recommendations for managing conflicts? select all that apply, then select submit. focus on issues focus on issues focus on issues focus on issues focus on issues focus on issues focus on the past and present focus on the past and present focus on the past and present focus on the past and present focus on the past and present focus on the past and present focus on time focus on time focus on time focus on time focus on time focus on time search for alternatives together search for alternatives together search for alternatives together search for alternatives together search for alternatives together search for alternatives together focus on the present and future focus on the present and future focus on the present and future focus on the present and future focus on the present and future focus on the present and future search for options independently search for options independently search for options independently search for options independently search for options independently search for options independently

incorrect that is incorrect. please try again. try again try again try again try again try again try again incorrect you did not select the correct response. continue continue continue continue continue continue correct that's right! you selected the correct response. continue continue continue continue continue continue drag the correct response card to each scenario. think carefully—multiple cards may feel appropriate, but only one is the best fit. a high-performing team member has recently become unusually quiet in meetings. they’ve stopped contributing ideas and are avoiding follow-ups. you sense something is wrong, but they haven’t spoken up. a team member went above and beyond during the project’s final week—staying late, solving key technical issues, and mentoring junior staff behind the scenes. their efforts weren’t widely visible to the rest of the team. a disagreement between two key members during a sprint review turned into personal tension. since then, others seem to be walking on eggshells. collaboration has slowed. knowledge check - 5 recognize recognize incentivize teambuild listen

slidetranscripts.slidetranscript: <Document xmlns:xsi="http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema-instance" xmlns:xsd="http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema"> <Content> <Block> <Style /> <Span Text="In 1965, psychologist Bruce Tuchman identified that teams evolved through five different stages. Forming, storming, norming, performing, and adjourning. Tuchman didn't just have a flair for rhyming, although that makes his model easier to keep in mind. Navigating these stages effectively can be crucial to fostering a high-performing team. In the first stage, the team is introduced. Everyone is friendly, pleasant, even enthusiastic. This is the forming stage, when most team members are excited to begin the project and get to know the others. Soon, of course, reality kicks in. During the storming phase, challenges emerge. Conflicts arise, and the team members start asserting their ideas. Then, the groups start to align, noticing and appreciating each other's strengths. This is the norming stage, when the group starts to establish more effective ways of working together. Communication improves, and everyone is contributing and working smoothly as a cohesive unit. Eventually, the new team's members are confidently functioning at their best, working independently, yet cohesively to achieve their goals with efficiency. This is the performing stage, when everyone on the team is on the same page and driving ahead toward the final destination. In 1977, Tuchman expanded his model by adding a fifth state, adjourning. This phase is sometimes called mourning, as the team members have grown close and may feel a sense of loss when the project concludes. The point of having a team is to work together. Recognizing how teams stand in the process is key to helping your team evolve into a mature and highly productive team." /> </Block> </Content> </Document>
